title: T2 is confusing.
post text:
Is t2 caused by obesity? Does losing weight make you lose insulin resistance?

Does anyone know for sure? How then are there thin t2 diabetics?

Example: my husband was diagnosed t2 at age 39. He's always taken meds since, mostly metformin.

He was heavy for a time (275 lbs) but has been thin for a few yrs (180 lbs, 5ft 11 in)

Last a1c was 8.1 despite meds. His dad's whole side is t2. He is 67.

Me: was 400 lbs 7 yrs ago due to severe sleep apnea. A1c was 6.9 at the highest.

Sleep apnea got treated, appetite hormones ghrelin and leptin normalized, and I lost all desire for foods with sugar/starches.

I easily dropped over 200 lbs which has stayed off. My last a1c was 5.6? No meds ever, only dietary change.  I am 65.

I've tested my BG frequently. Today I tested with a slice of my son's tomato pie, probably one of the worst things next to rice for blood sugar.

An hr later: 141. I find nowadays that no matter what I eat as a test, my BG never goes above the 140 range.

7 years ago eating something like that would put me into the mid 200s.

I have permanently changed my lifestyle to be zero sugar, fresh  berries the only fruit, only meats/non-starchy vegs, zero ultra-processed foods incl condiments, only water to drink. My tastes changed to the point where I prefer eating this way.

Is my diet/lifestyle why my BG doesn't spike crazy even after a carb? So does diet normalize BG or losing weight or both? If its weight loss, how is it that some t2s are thin?

title: “ Forbidden Rice “
post text:
I was recently at a private gathering a weekend ago and having been diagnosed with T2 mid-November I’ve been on point with making the important changes and making the various initial appointments and titrating the new meds in my life.

In general I kept things fairly balanced prior and I had gotten really good at making my own take on the “ Butter Chicken “ Indian dish which I had been serving over a coconut-Basmati rice. Yum!!

( and had gotten thrifty with my rice cooker/ steamer and a 20lb bag of Basmati rice at a steal)

I’ve been told as long as I keep the dish fairly balanced with protein and veggies that I can enjoy it as I have been but have been cautious with the carbs ever since.


Well, I may have found the most secret of all the various types of rice globally as well as finally finding out the secret of my SIFU’s purple veggie fried rice.

At this recent gathering we were talking while cleaning up and making some Togo plates with the leftovers and BAMB! The secret was revealed!

Only one place we have locally to get it AND I have done follow up research.

There are a few off type versions and some hybrid, but if your look up “ Purple Rice “ and “ Forbidden Rice “ you will find that it makes up the smallest version of rice globally and was originally kept and or served to the Emperor and family.

It’s super low glucose, has a nice texture and taste. Is very filling and specifically is naturally designed for those of us in the T2D community.

I didn’t ask about the cost locally but I will be making a trip over there in the next couple of days.

title: Creeping a1C - Intermittent Fasting?
post text:
I'd been IR and pre diabetic for years. Went to the doctor when I started peeing a lot and got a yeast infection that wouldn't go away with treatment.

My a1C on diagnosis was 113 mmol/mol (12.5%). In three months with diet and Ozempic I got it down to 46 mmol (6.4%).

Now I've been steadily creeping up again. Earlier in the year my a1C was 48 (6.5) and a few weeks ago it was 51 (6.8). This is only just under the maximum of what is considered ok management of diabetes.

So, because I had surgery for something else and my stomach was not well, I started intermittent fasting not by choice 16 hours fasting 8 eating. I've now got a fasting glucose of 6.9 (which wasn't tested last time I had blood tests for diabetes but the time before it was 8.4). Has anyone else had success with intermittent fasting? (Asking for shared experiences not medical advice.)

I just want to get off all the medication. I've also got Graves disease and ADHD and I take ridiculous amounts of medication and vitamins. Like I'm talking 15 pills per day. And I recently had a bit of a scare that my kidney function is declining, although not enough proteins in my blood or urine for a chronic kidney disease diagnosis.

title: Sharing My Journey So Far
post text: I had gone to the ER for acute pancreatitis after having severe abdominal pain and it felt like absolute torture. I was hospitalized and given iv fluids until my labs got better. I was told my A1C was at 11.3 when I was admitted in June. After about 3-4 days I was told I could go home but to start on Jardiance as soon as I get home. I take this medication for the next 3 days and I feel extremely fatigued, shortness of breath, weak, dizzy and hardly eating. I then projectile vomited one morning and couldn't stop. I immediately called my mom and my dad took me to the hospital. I was then told I went into some type of acidosis and needed to stay again until I got normal blood results. I got better and since then, I was doing okay up until December 12 and I was starting to feel lightheadness every now and then with some headaches. I wasn't checking my blood sugars the past month at all and started eating horribly. I then had gone to the gym to maybe help out and it made it worse. I felt like time was slowing down when I was on the treadmill and my heart rate was getting high. I felt like I couldn't breathe and started panicking. I called 911 and they told me my blood sugar was 247. I told them I would have my parents take me if it got worse and see how I felt after some time. Things didn't get better. I felt so overwhelmed and felt like breaking down and then I believe I had a panic attack because my dad took me to the hospital and they did some blood work, I had calmed down after an hour or two and I was told it was due to some symptoms that Diabetes can cause. The doctor told me my A1C is at an 8.4. I was happy to hear this but also wondered what was going on with my body. I was given Metformin 500mg with every meal. I took the metformin and it helped with lowering my fasting blood sugar and my blood sugar after eating was controlled. However, I felt lightheaded, anxiety, nausea, brain fog and everything felt like I was hyper aware. I did not like this one bit. I was dizzy most of the day and night. I remember taking the metformin after one bite of eating some food. I was also feeling shortness of breath. I stopped taking it and I feel okay now. It's been about 2 weeks since I stopped taking it and I've managed to bring my blood sugars after having a meal around 125-145. I did have a meal yesterday which was 2 ounces of ground turkey with low-skim mozzarella, cauliflower rice, and some raw carrots and brocolli. After two hours, my blood sugar was 88. This has never happened before and I was beginning to feel the lightheadness again, my face felt hot and hands felt cold. I decided to eat some saltine crackers and felt better afterwards. I'm hoping on January 3rd when I have my physical, they can check my A1C and see what it is.

title: Couldn't manage prediabetes, now I'm here.
post text:
Hi everyone, I guess I'm part of this subreddit now. As the title says, I knew I had prediabetes for years (I'd say around 8-10 years) as I am obese with a lot of extra weight. 33F, 140kg (308 lbs) last time I went to the doctor, Jan 21st. Fasting glucose of 136 mg/dL, and HbA1C of 7.3 - the last time I had bloodwork done it was 110 mg/dL and 6.0 in 2023, almost 2 years ago. 

I've struggled with my weight all my adult life, definitely some emotional/psychological issues having to do with comfort eating, binge eating, depression, anxiety and maybe undiagnosed ADHD. Both my grandmothers had diabetes T2, one died at 67 and the other's still alive and 94, so hey maybe the outlook isn't so bad for me. 

When I was first told I was prediabetic I remember going on a diet, eating better, losing some weight and getting blood glucose under control, so the doctor didn't worry too much and told me to keep it going. I would yoyo diet a lot, last time (2019?) I went from 123kg to like 110kg, I was doing keto diet but I was miserable keeping it up. 

When covid hit, I gained a of of that back, in 2021 I had gone back up to 126kg, but I got covid and lost my sense of smell and taste so I was able to go down to 115kg or so thanks to that lol. Then I got in a relationship and gained a lot of weight again, I would try to eat healthy but my binge/comfort eating continued, there's been a couple stressful life events having to do with family in there, as well as being lazy and ordering out a lot, on top of switching to a fully remote WFH job, so I ended up where I'm at now.

I knew something was wrong because my acanthosis nigricans was just getting worse (on my neck), I had BV as well as a yeast infection that wouldn't go away, lots of thirst and therefore peeing a lot, dry mouth, so I decided to get bloodwork done and here are the results. I've been on ozempic for about 2 months now so I don't know if that skews the results or not. Doctor prescribed me forxiga to help manage it. 

So I always thought I could just eat healthy and revert the prediabetes numbers, I thought "Oh, I'm just going on vacation for x days, then I'll get back on track" "Oh, it's just the weekend, diet back on on Monday", you get it. 

Well, now there's no more of that since now diabetes is forever - is it a blessing in desguise? It's easier to not have something if you know it's bad for you like that, at least in my case.

I feel like such a failure for not doing anything about this sooner or taking it seriously. Like I'm going to lose out on something in life (yet it's just food, it sounds kind of stupid) or that I put myself at risk - like getting covid again I could easily die or something. 

I've been eating way better in these 9 days since I got my blood work done, and also having light work outs and I've already lost about 3kg. Definitely a lot of water weight I'm assuming, but it gives me hope. I will definitely look into therapy/psych evaluation for the extra emotional support. 

So... anybody that got some kind words, I'd appreciate. Any hope that you can give me - to make this road easier. I'd love to hear what has worked for you. I still have to follow up with my dr. in about 3 weeks but I'd like to hear from others.

Did you have to stop diet/zero sugar drinks as well? (Like Coke Zero)

Are you full on no carbs? No pasta/noodles (even the Brown kind)? No rice? (Brown or basmati) 

What about any indulgence like pizza? (Assuming you'd eat it with a side salad or something)

Do you have to have a glucose monitor? The one that is on 24/7? Is that really necessary?

Am I at risk of hypoglycemia just for having T2?

Am I immunocompromised forever, or should I be alright once my blood sugars are in order?

For the women, have you been able to have children without too many complications? I haven't made a decision on children but I'm heading towards no, not because of T2 necessarily.

title: I genuinely think that NNC2215 is the most probable cure.
post text:
Hi guys, so if you didn't know, from my understanding NNC2215 is a modified insulin molecule that "activates" and "deactivate" itself based on the glucose level in its environment. The mechanism is explained in a video from a guy who helped create it and I don't think I'm capable of doing it justice. anyway, in my personal opinion this is the closest thing we can for a cure.  I think that probably the most important part of this "cure" is that its not a one time solution, which means that we still need to continuously spend money and that is the best outcome for most pharmaceutical companies.

second of all, I think its genuinely the easiest and safest way to be "cured", if somehow they can make the effect duration very long, it will be just like injecting long-acting insulin every 24h, no surgery needed and no immunosuppressants. 

by the way, for those wondering how deactivating insulin prevents hypoglycemia: (correct me if I'm wrong) from my very basic understanding, our alpha cells(glucagon secretors) are completely fine and they react when glucose is below a certain threshold. if insulin is still active during this time(because normally it is when you inject too much) it inhibits this reaction. deactivating it will make your alpha cells work normally, preventing lows. 

so what do you think?

title: Is this normal?
post text:
So I was diagnosed Type 2 about 2 months ago. Prior to my diagnosis, and the symptom that lead me to speak with my doctor, was significant weight loss that was unexpected and not as a result of any weight loss effort or intention on my part (despite needing it). 
 
 When I was tested, my A1C was 11.5. My most recent test prior to that was about 9 months prior and an A1C of 5.7, just barely in the pre-diabetic range.
 
 As a result, my doctor prescribed me 2000 mg of metformin daily, taken in 1000 mg extended release doses twice a day.
 
 I cut virtually all processed sugars and carbs as many T2s do and obsess about. However, now my blood sugar is constantly testing in the low 90s, and when I have a recent “cheat meal” (a burger from Applebees with a white bread bun with side of steamed broccoli) my sugars only went up to 112.
 
 Lastly, I had waited 2-3 weeks after my diagnosis and lifestyle changes to begin regularly testing my blood sugar, so I don’t know what my levels were leading up to my tests. I did however experience some very strong vision side effects from the shock to my system with suddenly much less sugar, like not being able to read and see clearly up close (this has since resolved on its own).
 
 My questions are:
 
 1) is it normal to be able to lower my levels so quickly? My A1C was so high, corresponding to an average blood sugar of nearly 300 mg/dL. That suggests intense initial insulin resistance, and I wouldn’t expect to get it under control so quickly. Some days I question the validity of my A1C lab test, but then I remember how the symptoms I experienced shortly after making changes were very much in-line with sudden drastic decreases in blood sugar levels.
 
 2) To those of you who have achieved T2 remission, how quickly were you able to? If you’re comfortable sharing some details of timelines and weight loss that would be helpful. My weight before I started experiencing diabetic caused weight loss was about 370 lbs (6 foot 4 male, later 20s). when I was diagnosed I was about 315. I’m currently 290. I want to lose a significant amount more, but based of weight loss and my current sugar levels, I want to know if remission is a realistic part of my future?
 
 Part of why I’m trying to get under control so quickly, other than just for my health, is because I need to pass an extremely, life altering medical exam in the somewhat near future, and uncontrolled diabetes is an automatic disqualifying factor.
 
 I’m hopeful but also somewhat ignorant because of how recent this all is for me. I realize diabetes is complex and any advice and insight would be greatly appreciated.

title: I now have a plan
post text:
So I've recently been diagnosed as a type 2 diabetic. Know it was coming as I have three immediate family members who are t2 and with the way my health is going downhill I knew it was a matter of time.

I've spoken to my GP and my bariatric dietitian, between us we have come up with a plan.

I am to carry on with the diet plan the dietitians and surgeon has agreed and set for me, my GP is on board with this. The dietitians also asked the diabetic team at the hospital there to see if they would make any changes and they signed off on my current plan.

I have been prescribed metformin and a plan to increase to maximum dose.

I will be referred for diabetic eye screening as I already wear glasses and my eyesight has gotten worse in the last week years.

He will see if the diabetic team at the hospital will take me on, but genrally they will say to GP manage for now, unless I progressively get worse. 

GP has sent an email to the lymphoedema specialist about this diagnosis to see if they will bring me up the list. 

Asked about finger prick tests and he said he will ask my regular GP to see if they want me to do that (the GP I spoke to is new to the role here and used to work in private care so it's diffrent here).

I will be reviewed in 6 months time to see how I'm doing.  

Exercise was mentioned but then after reading about my mobility issues he said to not push myself more then I absolutely have to, the only way I can walk for a short period of time is if I am dosed up on pain medication, which non of us want. 

Chasing up respiratory and head and neck clinic for my ?emphysema and the growth on my thyroid restricting my breathing. 

Another diagnosis to add to my list, it's hell but hopefully I can sort this and get into remission before my bariatric surgery.

title: Anyone else finding Kamut + butter bread helps keep blood sugars steady?
post text:
Hey folks, I wanted to drop in and share something that’s been working surprisingly well for me. I’ve been experimenting with my bread recipes to keep those post-meal spikes in check, and I’ve landed on a combo that’s been awesome: Kamut flour and butter.
 
 # My Method
 
 1. **Sub in Kamut Flour**: If your recipe calls for, say, 3 cups of flour, try replacing 1 cup with Kamut at first to see how you like the flavor and texture. You can always work up to 100% Kamut.
 2. **Don’t Skimp on Butter**: Besides adding it into the dough, I butter a slice when it’s fresh out of the oven. It’s such a game-changer for both taste *and* possibly the blood sugar situation.
 
 Anybody else tried Kamut bread? Let me know if it helps your glucose control—or if you have your own secret ingredient. Always looking for new ways to keep my levels steady without sacrificing good bread!

title: How long do people usually continue with finger-stick testing?
post text:
Not looking for advice, just trying to understand the usual progression of things. I was diagnosed late July 2023 with a lab a1c of 11.3. Spend ten days in hospital getting everything checked out, three months on insulin injections and metformin with a CGM. My a1c dropped to 5.8-6.0 in that time so they took me off the insulin, and insurance (Japanese *kokumin hoken* national health) doesn't cover CGM for people not on insulin. Since then I've been on two finger sticks a day (first thing in the morning and around 5pm) and just got word that my a1c is down to 5.5. Yay me, but like a fool I forgot to ask the doctor if I still needed to do finger sticks. 

Is this the sort of thing that people generally stop needing once they understand healthy/healthier eating and your meds are calibrated, or is it something that most T2 diabetics end up doing forever? I had the worst condition going in of anyone I personally know with diabetes, but right now I'm doing better than they are. It's a very small sample set and I'd rather not ask my doc foolish questions (there's a language barrier, my Japanese isn't so hot, so I like to have a good idea of the likely answers before I go in babbling questions). 

Thanks in advance, and again, not looking for advice on how long *I* need to do this, just experiences of whether it's something most people are stuck with or something that might eventually be phased out.

title: How to Support Husband with Type 2
post text:
Hi all. My husband (43m) was diagnosed with type 2 in 2022 after sudden weight loss, exhaustion, frequent urination etc. At that time his numbers were high 400s, A1C was 11 and he got sick shockingly fast. We were able to get his numbers back down to the 100-200 range very quickly with Metformin and diet changes, and he has been pretty stable since then, until the birth of our son in December. 
 
 We didn’t realize how much lack of sleep affects insulin sensitivity until it was too late - now his body is wasting away again and his sugars are in the high 300 to high 400 range. His doctor put him on a CBGM and added Rybelsus to his meds but he is still high most of the time and losing weight fast. I’ve taken over all night feedings and try to get baby out of the bedroom before he cries enough to wake my husband, but I don’t know what else to do to help. When he first got sick I dropped everything and got him on a 2 hour eating schedule and vetted everything he was eating for a few weeks to get him well. I can’t really do that now with the baby, but I can tell he still doesn’t have as good a handle on what to eat as I do. 
 
 Any new parents with similar experiences? Or any advice on how to get his sugars under control again fast? I hate seeing him so thin and frail looking and weak when he is normally a big, strong, virile person. I just want my husband well again.

title: GLP1s
post text:
I just wanted to say to anybody that needs to hear it: there is no shame in using a GLP1. It’s a tool. It helps regulate a hormonal and metabolic food noise (among other things) that can help you make sustainable choices that facilitate healthy outcomes.

I see a lot of people in this subreddit talking about “doing it the natural way” but that’s crap.  Natural is being able to rely on your level hormones to make eating decisions about when you’re hungry but not everyone has that system functioning properly. I am a driven woman, have accomplished many things in my life already and waiting for my body to understand satiety was not going to happen. It wasn’t willpower, I climbed freaking Machu Picchu — I have willpower. It was a fight I couldn’t win without the help of Mounjaro.

If you don’t want to white-knuckle your diet the rest of your life in a losing battle, consider asking your doctor about it.  It’s not going to be a good fit or right match for everyone (and of course ALL meds have risks) but I think that some people, myself included and I will die on this hill, are not capable (physically) of maintaining the type of eating that so many “normal” people seem to do so easily. This medicine can be a game changer. 

It was for me. 

(A1c from 11.9 to 5.5, weight from 240lbs to 140lbs, 40yr F)

title: Managing blood sugar through intense cardio / lifting
post text:
I’m a 20 year old male who was diagnosed with T1D at age 4. I’ve played plenty of sports and worked out throughout life and have never really had a problem (excluding a few times of course) with managing my levels. 

However, due to my schedule, I have had to move my cardio and weight lifting to the same session: I warm up with semi-intensive cardio for ~45 minutes before lifting 6 days a week. I used to do cardio after, but switched it due to hypertrophy reasons as well as a rec from my endo. 

I am now really struggling - even if my blood sugar is say, 200 when I start cardio, it always ends up between 50-70 by the end of the session, or drops even lower. Even with no carbs, it will then often be back at 200 or even higher after my lifting (~1 hour or so). While this is somewhat expected given the general effects of aerobic vs resistance training, it makes it really hard to A) lift with a low blood sugar for the first half of my workout, B) know when to eat something when my blood sugar goes low only to have it skyrocket later, and C) have to do a reactionary injection during / after my workout.  

I am thinking of adding 1-2 units either right before or immediately following cardio, but I am nervous that this will make me too low without realizing with no spike after. 

If anyone has any recommendations or similar experience please let me know!

My Kahlua:

1 Cup Lacanto Brown Monkfruit

1 Cup Anthony's Allulose granulated sugar

1/2 Cup Espresso, Moka Pot coffee, or some very strong brewed coffee

1/8 Cup Vanilla

2 Cups Vodka or Low Carb Rum

title: PSA for Women
post text:
Your menstrual cycle affects your insulin sensitivity and thus your blood glucose. When I look at my glucose and see that my fasting is higher than normal or my 3+ hours after a meal that I know I’ve dosed as I always would (I tend to eat the same breakfast everyday), all I have to do is check my Flo App and sure enough I’m around 7-8 days away from my period. It’s like clockwork. 

Typically for me, the week leading up to my period, I get quite a few premenstrual symptoms and some level of insulin resistance. I will increase my basal by 2U around this time to counteract this. This is known as the luteal phase.

The week my period starts and up to a few days into my follicular phase, I’m usually extra insulin sensitive so I’ll drop down by 4U.

Then, in between is my “normal”. I’ll increase by 2U from what it was to my menstruation basal.

For me, this means a range of 8U to 12U depending on where I am on my cycle:
Luteal phase (week leading up to menstruation): 12U
Week of period up to few days into follicular phase: 8U
“Normal”: 10U

So, if you are feeling you seem to be doing worse at managing and everything is spiking you up the wazoo when you haven’t changed anything, check your cycle! That’s usually the culprit for me.

title: Eye Pain After Eating

Right after I eat most things, I get pain on the sides/corners of my eyes, my eyes become bloodshot, I feel tired/sleepy, I get very slightly blurred vision, and sometimes I get a headache. Is it possible that what I am eating is initially causing a spike of 30 points or more and even if it comes back down to normal after 2 hours, the symptoms are from the spike?

I have been to my primary care doctor, gastroenterologist, and endocrinologist and they all say if your blood sugar numbers are normal 2 hours after eating and you are not experiencing extreme thirst or frequent urination than your symptoms are not from high blood sugar.

Does anyone experience similar symptoms or might know what is causing this or what is going on?

title: Did most of you know that they lowered the A1c numbers back in the mid 90s?
post text:
The American Diabetes Association (ADA) lowered the recommended A1c target to below 7% for most patients with diabetes in the mid-to-late 1990s, marking a significant shift in treatment guidelines; however, more recent recommendations from the American College of Physicians (ACP) suggest a more individualized approach with an A1c target between 7 and 8% for many patients with type 2 diabetes, depending on individual factors like life expectancy and medication risks. 
Key points about A1c guideline changes:
Lowering of A1c target:
The ADA significantly lowered the A1c target to below 7% in the mid-to-late 1990s. 
Industry influence:
Some argue that pharmaceutical companies heavily influenced this change by promoting drugs that could achieve lower A1c levels. 
Recent shift towards individualization:
The ACP recently published guidelines recommending a more individualized approach with an A1c target between 7 and 8% for many type 2 diabetes patients. 

No, the question is how do you feel when you’re at a six point something I remember in the 90s when they diagnosed me for diabetes and I got myself down to 6.1 they were like congratulations you effectively lowered your A1c out a diabetic range

And then one day, I remember the doctor going in you’re out of range when I was a 6.9

I was like wow what happened and he said wow they changed it 

So the question how do you feel when you’re in the sixes

title: Genuine question about low anxiety
post text:
Without sharing my entire story, since it is a confusing one... basically this. Been diabetic for like 9 ish years now. Probably going on a decade if i remember the day i was diagnosed right.

For a while, when i was younger, i didn't really care for the severity of diabetes. I mostly just ate salads and called it a day. some years after i kinda "forgot" which is stupid, i know. More like i felt i was doing good enough to sort of put it on the back burner because my sugars were just fine for so long after committing to nothing but salad.

Anyways moving to today, my sugars steady went up as i stopped eating exclusively salads. and these days as an adult im way more health conscious. BUT to a degree that is harming me. I keep getting slightly uncomfortable with lower, or what i should call normal blood sugar, which i consider about 120 mg/dl. But even when its going down, and its slowly correcting going from 200 -> 180 -> 165 -> etc. I start getting nervous around 150 mg/dl.

Now i just can't beat this anxiety that "Oh no my sugar is going down... what if it doesn't stop going down?" etc. Then i eat like 12g of carbs via glucose tabs, watch my sugars slowly drop more (because i only wait like 5-10 mins instead of the full 15 between checks...)

im just a mess with it. Thankful for my doctors, but i do want to ask a community of others with diabetes. have you felt this? have you beat it? I know its just me being used to being high sugars for so long. But i get so worried when its going down to normal levels and my body is trying to 'adjust' and in the process im CAUSING anxiety that mimic blood sugar lows. thus fueling my anxiety causing me to over correct when not even needed. stuff like clammy hands and maybe a tiny bit of shakes.

I just can't stop this fear that if feel any side effect of a low, and my checks are confirming its dropping, it will just keep dropping and i wont be able to react quick enough to fix it before i go unconscious or something. (PS even typing this out helped me get a little under control. I was typing this as i saw my sugar go from 125 (check 1) -> 120 (check 2) -> 113 -> (check 4). I took 4mg of glucose tabs so i can rest assure i have SOMETHING 'fighting' the feeling of the "low"

edit: feel like people are skim reading and then assuming the entire ordeal. I'm aware that 150 isnt low. I know 120 mg/dl isnt low. Whole point was me saying I get anxiety when it's going to a normal level because I'm so used to sitting at high blood sugar I'm "correcting" at that level because I feel scared and as if it is getting lower. title: Recently diagnosed, spent almost 5 days in hospital
post text:
Almost 32 here, was actively trying to change my health over summer. Down from 385 (July) to 335 (November). Started Ozempic and Lisinopril the same day in November.

Began communication with my health team, got a BP cuff etc. My vision began blurring several times a day, bright lights couldn't see properly. Got bad enough one day I couldn't see 30 feet infront of me on highway or the median lines. 

One doctor moved an appointment to rush me in. Went in. Discussed some stuff, off one med and did blood work. 

My fiance called my work a few hours later as I wasn't answering my phone (just busy working). Blood results back- manager tells me I need to go to ER. 

Long story short, Blood sugar levels above 800. Entire ER was shocked I was coherent much less driving. Said I should have been in a coma or having seizures. 

Spent the past week getting picked and pumped full of insulin. Triglycerides very high but are lowering. Finally got discharged. 

Just new to this I guess. Blood sugars still high, hovering between 180 and 250 depending on time. 
Almost nervous for breakfast and retest in a few hours...on a low everything diet. Just going to have some cheerios and almond milk.

title: Small Win!
post text:
I was diagnosed with pre-diabetes a couple of years ago and became diabetic a year ago. I was then prescribed metformin, which I was very bad at taking since it gave me a lot of GI issues..

Fast forward to end of Sept, my diabetes got worse.. my A1C was at 9.8. I finally went to an endocrinologist to help me with diabetes management. My doctor is so reassuring. He let me know that my A1C of 9.8 is not the end of the world. He put me on Ozempic (0.25-0.5mg/wk) and had me keep taking my metformin. I also subscribed to one of those monthly CGM subscriptions so that I can monitor my blood sugar 24/7.

Well, it’s been two months since my initial appt with my endocrinologist and my lab works are out from yesterday.. my A1C is 6.7! I went from 9.8 to 6.7 in 2 months. I am so happy. I even went to Spain a few weeks ago for vacation and ate carbs.

I just want to say thank you to everyone who contributes here on Reddit. It’s really my number one source of inspiration.

Now.. I’m trying to figure out how to eventually wean off meds but still manage my diabetes. I’ve cut out simple carbs from my daily diet and switched them to more complex carbs. I don’t do any added sugar. I’ve cut out fruits. I’ve also limited the amount of carbs I eat. I don’t want to do keto. How did you get off meds?

title: Low Carb Seafood Soup Lunch
post text:
Eating low carb and healthy is more about preparation than time and complexity. This was a lunch I threw together in 30 minutes with things i had in my pantry, fridge and freezer. 

Popped open two quarts of my seafood and shellfish stock, that i make myself and can, grabbed some sable fish, tuna, salmon, prawns, and lobster from the freezer. Chopped up a jalapeno, half of a tomato, green onion, cilantro, cabbage that i had in the fridge. Added the rest of some leftover mushroom soup and a cup of Chardonnay (last of an open bottle). Grated some old ginger i had shriveled up in my cupboard. 

Simmered the ingredients for 20 minutes. Added the lobster and prawns first. Cooked at a simmer for 5 minutes, added the sable fish, tuna. Shut-off the heat, added the salmon. Don't forget to season everything before it goes in with salt! 

Filled the bowls with angel hair shirataki noodles (no cooking needed and 4 grams TOTAL carbs mostly fiber), arranged the green onion, cilantro, sambal olec, and seafood. Ladled the broth on top.

Very low carb lunch is served! High in protein, vitamins, trace minerals and NOTHING ultra processed. I took a half hour to prepare this, but could have been done in 15 minutes. Can also be prepared in advance and reheated in 5 minutes.

title: Have you switched meds despite good control, and would you do it again
post text:
Not looking for medical advice, but for context, I was diagnosed just under a year ago by my GP who prescribed Janumet. Starting A1c was 10.6 - I took the meds and cut my carbs and after three months I got it down to 6.1, after six months 5.3, after nine months still sitting at 5.6.

Had my first Endo appointment last week. He had no concerns with my current level of control but suggested switching medication to either Jardiance or Ozempic, citing their increased effectiveness and cardiovascular benefits over Januvia/Janumet. 

In the end I said I would think about it and we agreed to revisit the idea in a few months time. In the mean time, I'm sampling a CGM to gather a bit more data about just how controlled I am.

On one hand, I am very much of the mindset that, if it ain't broke, don't fix it - save the stronger meds until a time comes that I am losing control and actually need them. 

But on the other side I can see how the newer meds  may help me obtain even better control, perhaps with a little bit less effort on the diet front, and over the long term that may have its own benefits. 

So, I assume at least some of you have been in a similar situation, wondering how you handled it and if you have any regrets about switching or not switching.

title: Unusual Case
post text:
My husband was diagnosed with T2 in Sept after going into DKA. He had normal labs in June, but had been experiencing a wide range of symptoms all year that the doctors never tied to diabetes. They included:

1) insomnia
2) stomach pain after eating
3) head pressure after eating 
4) panic attacks after eating

Because of his labs our GP didn’t suspect diabetes and sent him to a neurologist and GI doctor. All the tests they ran (brain MRI, colonoscopy and endoscopy) were normal. We were so frustrated. It wasn’t until 3 days before he went into DKA that he showed the usual symptoms of increased thirst and frequent urination. We went to an urgent care and his blood glucose was too high to even register so they sent us to the ER. He was in the ICU for two days and a regular room for two more. He started off taking 15unit of short acting insulin with meals and 50units long acting at night. His numbers immediately improved and now less than 3 months later he’s only on 4units of long acting at night and metformin twice a day. 

All of his symptoms have gone away. He’s like a new man. He sleeps great, he’s lost 30lbs, he’s no longer suffering. The diagnosis gave him a new lease on life.

His endocrinologist thought he might have LADA but further work ups showed he has “Flatbush diabetes” or ketosis-prone diabetes, which has both T1 and T2 characteristics. They usually have a sudden onset of DKA and respond really well to insulin. 

All this to say, be careful of unusual symptoms and don’t dismiss them. Insist on regular labs. Don’t give up.

title: If you take/took Metformin, but also have sleep apnea, tap in… I want to see something.
post text:
Okay so first of all, let me start by saying that I'm not a doctor, just a patient who is nerdy and likes to stay informed about my health. 

I have a theory about something, and I guess I just wanted to see if it tracks. 

My theory: People with untreated/uncontrolled sleep apnea will (possibly) not tolerate Metformin well because sleep apnea raises your level of lactic acid and so does MF, thus creating more than your system can clear at once and causing symptoms like lethargy and confusion. 

If you couldn't tolerate Metformin well, and then later found out you had sleep apnea.... tap in. 

This theory is unfortunately molded by real life experience. I couldn't tolerate Metformin, my lactic acid was borderline high prior to taking it, my doc suspects i have sleep apnea. 

I'm just wondering if anyone else found a correlation between difficult side effects and SA!

title: An encouraging update for the newly diagnosed. It gets easier, but a lot depends on attitude.
post text:
For anybody who didn’t see my recent backstory post it’s here: 



TL;DR - 46, male, no prior health issues whatsoever, had three heart attacks in quick succession, almost died, had heart surgery, and was finally diagnosed with diabetes- very strong genetic component on my mother’s side. 

So the first three weeks have been *interesting* to say the least. 

I can no longer have 8 pints of Guinness with my pals on a Friday night, my beloved mounds of fluffy Irish potatoes with butter and salt are gone forever, and the rows of biscuits and crisps on shop shelves now audibly laugh as I walk by…

I was so depressed the first week after leaving hospital that I went into autopilot and thought of food as just medicine. I hated it. I hated preparing it, eating it, and even thinking about it. 

A typical lunch became a plate of microwaved frozen veg with a lump of plain chicken breast and some kind of weird homemade sauce thing. Get up, prick my finger, measure glucose, eat horrific boring crap. 

I was angry, resentful, and didn’t want to speak to anybody. 

 “Nobody has a clue what I’m going through” was my new identity. And the truth is most people really don’t get it. Some do; some friends and family sympathise…but most don’t have a clue. And that’s just how it is. 

But the constant reminder I gave myself was that I’m young and **there’s nothing I can do about this**. It took time, but that constant realisation worked for me- “This is it, Dave; the new normal. Get used to it”. 

My blood sugar is nowhere near normal yet but my body is still adjusting. It’s been through an intense shock and my digestive system is still trying to accommodate the huge changes in diet. Nobody even warns you about the latter! 

Two things I highly recommend are sprinkling ground linseed (flaxseed) on absolutely everything - for the fibre and remarkable health benefits. The second is to drink more water than you previously drank. Ground linseed is incredibly cheap, has no taste, and the scientific articles on it basically talk about it as though it’s the elixir of life. Your guts will thank you for using the stuff. 

But more generally, I’ve realised that my attitude to all this is what will determine how successful I am in managing this crappy disease for the rest of my time here. There are people out there struggling to find food at all, struggling to find a scrap of bread to feed their kids, yet there I was whining about having to eat chicken breast in low-fat mayonnaise instead of a beef burger and fries. 

Attitude is going to play a colossal role in my self-treatment, and it will in yours too. 

No doubt I’ll have moments where I’ll just want to binge on a mountain of spuds and butter, but I’ll deal with it. Even the change in my social life has been minimal- I go to the pub, sip my one Guinness Zero, still have a laugh, and don’t miss the hangovers one iota. I could’ve made a massive deal about that but what’s the point? I’m better off not getting plastered every weekend anyway! 

The entire point is that you’re capable of far more adaptation than you realise, and that gratitude will get you much further in any health crisis than anger and resistance. It is what it is, so look for all the positives- there are far more than you first realise. 

Give it time, be gentle on yourself, accept reality. 

It all works out.
label: yes stigma

title: Accepting and working with my situation
post text:
I was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes last November, I am 24. It’s been hard, from just asking myself how this could happen, to even denying it to an extent (more like not accepting it), to even disregarding it. I also had a heart transplant when I was 16 and the weight of the long term ramifications of that plus the Diabetes was a lot, at times I felt “who cares, if I die I die”. Plus I have ADHD, so it’s easy to slip into hopelessness and feel so overwhelmed at times. 
 
 Anyways, with the help of therapy, I realized that I can’t let this bring me down. Admittedly I also saw someone describe diabetes as your body rusting from the inside and that also put it into perspective how bad it was, even if I should have known how bad it is. 
 
 Regardless, I want to face this and not run. I plan on talking with the doctor and using the available diabetes education to tackle this. I take metformin and my doctors are trying to fight my insurance to get Mounjaro approved. But I just came here looking for advice, words of encouragement, anything really. Tips on foods people have made a staple of their diet. How to keep yourself motivated. How to tackle situations where maybe you want a sweet treat. I would appreciate any help.
